NorthWest.riskExplained: The North West gets a lot more rainfall than almost anywhere else in England, which results in a high fluke and nematode risk. Wet ground persists well into spring and autumn, extending the time for parasites to lay eggs.

SouthWest.riskExplained: The South West is one of the highest risk regions in England for liver fluke, with mild winters and high rainfall keeping mud snail habitats wet for much of the year. Nematode presence is also evident through spring and autumn.

WestMidlands.riskExplained: The West Midlands sits in a mid-range risk bracket. Rainfall is moderate and conditions are generally favourable for nematode development through warmer months. Fluke risk is lower than the western regions but worth monitoring in wet years.

Yorkshire.riskExplained: Yorkshire sits in the middle — the upland western areas see higher rainfall and fluke presence, while the drier eastern plains carry lower risk. Nematode risk peaks in spring and late summer across most of the region.

EastMidlands.riskExplained: The East Midlands is generally lower risk than western regions, though nematode and coccidia presence remains in mixed farming areas. Liver fluke risk is low due to drier conditions.

EastAnglia.riskExplained: East Anglia is the driest region in England, which keeps most pasture parasite risk relatively low. Liver fluke is uncommon here. Nematodes can still be an issue in wetter summers, but overall burden is lower than western regions.

SouthEast.riskExplained: The South East is generally low to moderate risk. Drier summers keep fluke pressure low, though nematodes can build up during wetter periods. Blowfly strike risk is worth watching through the warmer months.

NorthEast.riskExplained: The North East sees moderate rainfall and cooler temperatures, which slightly compresses the parasite development window compared to southern regions. Nematode and fluke risk are both present, particularly in wetter upland areas.

ScotlandWest.riskExplained: Scotland West is one of the highest rainfall regions in the UK, creating persistently wet pasture conditions year-round. This significantly extends larval survival on pasture and keeps fluke habitats active for much of the year. Nematode risk is high through spring and autumn.

ScotlandEast.riskExplained: Scotland East is considerably drier than the west coast, with conditions more similar to northern England. Nematode risk is moderate and peaks in spring. Liver fluke risk is lower here but increases in wetter years or on poorly drained fields.

ScotlandNorth.riskExplained: Northern Scotland has a shorter parasite development season due to cooler temperatures and higher altitude. While rainfall is moderate, the cold winters limit larval survival on pasture. Risk peaks in the brief warmer months of late spring and summer.

ScotlandBorders.riskExplained: The Scottish Borders has moderate rainfall and mixed terrain. Upland areas carry higher fluke risk while lower ground sees more nematode pressure in spring and autumn. Risk levels are similar to northern England.

NorthWales.riskExplained: North Wales — particularly Gwynedd and Snowdonia — receives some of the highest rainfall in the UK. Persistently wet upland pastures create ideal conditions for liver fluke year-round. Nematode risk is also very high through spring and autumn.

SouthWales.source: Met Office station: Glamorgan CCC Cardiff 1991-2020 (1074.69mm)
SouthWales.riskExplained: South Wales has high rainfall and mild winters, keeping pasture conditions wet for much of the year. Liver fluke risk is significant, particularly on low-lying and poorly drained land. Nematode pressure is high through spring and autumn.

MidWales.riskExplained: Mid Wales receives high annual rainfall and has extensive upland grazing. Liver fluke is a major concern due to persistently wet conditions. Nematode risk is elevated through the main grazing season, particularly in spring.

EastWales.riskExplained: East Wales is drier than the rest of Wales but still carries moderate parasite risk. Nematode pressure is present through the grazing season and liver fluke risk increases in wetter years, particularly on heavier soils.

NorthernIrelandEast.riskExplained: Northern Ireland East has high rainfall and a mild Atlantic climate that keeps pastures wet for much of the year. Liver fluke risk is significant and nematode pressure is high through spring and autumn. The mild winters allow some larval survival year-round.

NorthernIrelandWest.source: Met Office Northern Ireland regional series 1991-2020 — west exceeds 1000mm
NorthernIrelandWest.riskExplained: Northern Ireland West is one of the highest risk areas in the UK for liver fluke, with very high rainfall, mild temperatures and low-lying wet ground providing ideal conditions for mud snails year-round. Nematode risk is also very high throughout the grazing season.
